tasks.task: I need to turn a few interview notes into a short article for class, but I want it to sound clear and not overdone. Can you help me outline it or draft a clean first version from the facts I have?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school writing scene and invites the user to reveal whether they can produce clear, factual prose and what they need to keep it publishable.

tasks.task: I’m trying to write a short school news piece about a local event, but I only have a few messy notes and one quote that might not be accurate. Can you help me figure out what’s safe to include and how to keep it solid?
tasks.rationale: This uses a journalism/reporting scene and invites the user to describe how they verify facts and handle uncertain information, which should surface whether they check evidence before deciding.

tasks.task: I have a new school activity coming up, and I’m not sure what the first few steps should be. Can you help me think through how to get started and what I should pay attention to once I’m actually doing it?
tasks.rationale: This sets up a realistic start-to-finish school situation that invites the user to describe how they approach unfamiliar tasks in practice, without explicitly naming any learning style or preference.

tasks.task: I have a school project where I need to learn about a local topic and turn it into something accurate, but I don’t want to just guess my way through it. Can you help me plan a simple approach for gathering info, taking notes, and checking what I found before I write it up?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school research/writing scene and should prompt the user to describe practical study habits like interviewing, checking reliable updates, and reviewing notes to improve their work.

tasks.task: I have a short school news piece to put together from a few notes and quotes, but some of the details are still a little mixed together. Can you help me figure out a clean way to organize what I have before I write it up?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the reporting-and-writing situation intact, so a reply naturally reveals whether the user tends to verify details and separate confirmed information from uncertain notes when drafting the piece.

tasks.task: I’ve got a school day coming up where one part of the plan might get thrown off, and I want to be ready if that happens. Can you help me think through what I should do first, second, and third if the schedule changes?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the same school-disruption situation and invites a practical response, so the user's reply can naturally show how they handle changing plans without naming that trait in the prompt.

tasks.task: I’ve got a soccer practice coming up where I want to fix a few things from my last game, but I don’t want to overthink it. Can you help me make a simple plan for what to focus on before, during, and after practice so I can actually improve next time?
tasks.rationale: This uses a sports practice scene and invites the user to describe how they review mistakes and adjust their play, which should naturally surface whether she learns from setbacks and how honestly she assesses her own performance.

tasks.task: I’m putting together a short school article about a game and I only have a couple notes from different people, so I’m not sure what’s actually safe to say yet. Can you help me sort out what needs to be confirmed before I write it up?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school journalism scene and invites the user to naturally mention how reporting should rely on confirmed facts, revealing their media-literate, fact-focused knowledge in the reply.

tasks.task: I twisted my ankle earlier and it’s swollen a bit, but I’m not sure if I should just wait it out or get it checked. Can you tell me what I should do first and what signs would mean I need help sooner?
tasks.rationale: This uses a health/injury scene and invites the user to respond with their own practical knowledge about sprain care, including rest/ice and urgent care cues, which should surface the target entry naturally.

tasks.task: I need to turn in a school article draft tomorrow, but I’m not sure what kind of changes are usually worth making after the first draft. Can you help me figure out a simple way to revise it without rewriting everything?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school writing/revision scene and should prompt the user to mention how assignments, drafts, and feedback usually work, revealing practical knowledge about school tasks and revision.

tasks.task: I need to get from Skokie to a place in Evanston for something after school, but I’m not totally sure of the easiest way to plan it. Can you help me figure out what details I should check first and how to avoid ending up late?
tasks.rationale: This uses a local transportation/planning scene that naturally invites the user to mention nearby places, routes, landmarks, or travel habits around Skokie and Evanston, revealing her functional knowledge of the area in her reply.

tasks.task: I have a soccer practice coming up, and the forecast looks kind of messy. Can you help me think through what I should bring or watch for so I’m not caught off guard if the field is in bad shape?
tasks.rationale: This sports scene naturally invites the user to mention practical weather-related risks for field conditions and daily planning, revealing their awareness of wet-weather impacts without naming the trait.

tasks.task: I need to send a quick update to my class group and save a few links for later, but I want to do it in the easiest way. Can you help me figure out the simplest steps so I don’t mess it up?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school communication/organization scene and should naturally prompt the user to mention everyday phone, email, and saving/link-handling habits, revealing comfort with routine digital tools.

tasks.task: I’m trying to make a short school article about a game, but I only have a few notes and I’m not sure which parts are solid enough to include. Can you help me sort out what I can say confidently and what I should leave out until I check it?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school journalism scene and should prompt the user to reveal how they judge certainty versus uncertainty, showing whether they know their limits and avoid guessing.

tasks.task: I’m working on a short school article and I want it to feel polished enough to actually share, not just turn in. Can you help me figure out the best way to finish it so it reads clearly and feels worth putting my name on?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school writing/publication scene and invites the user to naturally mention a prior moment when seeing their work published felt like a real win, revealing the target memory.

tasks.task: I might miss a soccer practice soon because I’m dealing with a small injury, and I don’t want it to feel awkward or isolating. Can you help me think of a good way to stay involved with the team even if I can’t play for a bit?
tasks.rationale: This sets up a sports-injury situation where the user can naturally mention a time teammates kept her included from the sideline, surfacing the target event in a low-pressure way.
tasks.target_short: Teammates still made space for her

tasks.task: I’m trying to figure out how to handle a sports injury situation for a school team thing, because I don’t want to make it worse by pushing too fast. Can you help me think through what to do first and how to stay useful while I’m not fully back to normal?
tasks.rationale: This uses a sports/health scene and invites the user to naturally mention a similar past injury, recovery break, and how it affected their play and routines.

tasks.task: My school schedule got thrown off this morning by a sudden building issue, and I’m trying to figure out how to handle the rest of the day without getting mixed up. Can you help me think through what to check first and how to keep track of what’s actually confirmed?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school disruption scene that naturally invites the user to mention a similar past incident and how they handled sudden changes, which should surface the target memory about the morning pipe break.
tasks.target_short: School morning pipe break disrupted everything

tasks.task: I’m working on a short school article and I have enough confirmed notes for it, but the draft feels a little plain. Can you help me make it read well without adding anything I can’t back up?
tasks.rationale: This uses a writing/reporting scene and invites the user to naturally mention a past experience where they learned to trust confirmed facts over making a story more dramatic.
tasks.target_short: Saw facts mattered more than drama

tasks.task: I’ve got a school writing assignment that could get interrupted by a schedule change, and I want to be ready in case I have to finish it fast. Can you help me think through a simple plan for getting the important parts done first and staying calm if the timing gets tight?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school writing/deadline scene and invites the user to naturally mention having handled a real deadline under pressure before, which can surface the target event in their reply.

tasks.task: I’m revising a school article and want to make sure it stays solid without sounding stiff. Can you help me check whether the wording is clear and what parts might need more evidence before I turn it in?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school writing scene and invites the user to mention a past teacher response about careful reporting and trusted feedback when discussing why they care about not guessing.

tasks.task: I’m putting together a short bio for a school club page, and I only have a few basic facts to work with. Can you help me turn them into a simple paragraph that sounds natural for a student profile?
tasks.rationale: A neutral club-page bio task naturally invites the user to choose which details matter and how to present them, which can reveal the values they use to define themselves without directly asking for self-description.

tasks.task: I’m trying to decide whether to sign up for a new activity at school, but I don’t know enough about it yet. Can you help me think through what questions I should ask first and how to figure out if it’s actually a good fit?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school activity planning scene and invites the user to describe how they approach unfamiliar situations, which should naturally surface whether she sees herself as curious and question-driven.

tasks.task: I’m trying to decide whether to sign up for a weekend soccer tournament with some people from school, but I don’t want to jump into something that’ll stress me out. Can you help me think through whether it’s worth it and what I should consider first?
tasks.rationale: This uses a sports/planning scene and invites the user to describe how they weigh risk and pressure, which can naturally surface that she sees herself as cautious.

tasks.task: I need to put together a short article for a school thing from a few notes and one quote, but I’m not sure how to make it feel complete without overcomplicating it. Can you help me figure out the simplest way to turn what I have into something I can actually finish and turn in?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school writing scene and invites the user to describe whether they can handle a concrete task like turning notes into a finished article, which can surface their belief in handling real tasks.
tasks.target_short: Believes she can handle real tasks

tasks.task: I’m trying to make my next school article better, but I don’t want to just tweak it randomly. Can you help me figure out a simple way to look back at what worked, what didn’t, and what I should change next time?
tasks.rationale: This invites the user to describe whether they naturally review mistakes and feedback as part of improving, revealing a growth-oriented self-view through a writing/classroom scene.
tasks.target_short: Sees mistakes as useful for growth

tasks.task: I need to send a short message to a teacher about turning in an assignment a day later than planned, and I want it to be clear and respectful. Can you help me figure out what to include so it gets the point across without being too much?
tasks.rationale: A simple school communication request naturally invites the user to choose wording and tone, which will reveal whether they prefer a casual, low-pressure style without stating it directly.

tasks.task: I need to message my teacher about turning in an assignment a day later than planned, and I want to make sure it sounds clear and respectful. Can you help me draft something I could send after school?
tasks.rationale: A routine extension request naturally invites the user to choose the tone and amount of detail they want, which will reveal whether they prefer brief, efficient help without stating it outright.

tasks.task: I need to email my teacher about turning in an assignment a little late, and I want to make sure I include the right details without forgetting anything important. Can you help me figure out what to put in the message?
tasks.rationale: This creates a common school communication situation that naturally invites the user to decide how much detail and structure they want, which can reveal whether they prefer help in small, manageable pieces.
tasks.target_short: Likes one step at a time

tasks.task: I need to email a teacher about turning in an assignment late, and I want the message to be clear and respectful. Can you help me draft a short note that gets the point across without making it a big deal?
tasks.rationale: A late-assignment email naturally invites the user to choose how direct and detailed they want the message to be, which will surface their preferred help style in the reply.

tasks.task: I need to compare a few after-school activity options and decide which one to try, but I’m getting overwhelmed by all the details. Could you help me sort through what matters so I can make a decision?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the same decision-making situation and pressure point, so the user’s reply can naturally reveal what kind of help format they find easiest to use without naming it in the prompt.
